name: agent
context: Start ssh-agent, add or remove keys with ssh-add, and manage agent-backed authentication safely
info: This skill covers the OpenSSH authentication agent workflow: starting `ssh-agent`, exporting `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` and `SSH_AGENT_PID`, loading keys with `ssh-add`, setting key lifetimes, requiring confirmation, listing and clearing loaded identities, and understanding the security tradeoffs of agent forwarding. Agents use it when private keys are passphrase-protected, when many SSH operations should reuse one unlocked key, or when a bastion hop needs temporary delegated authentication.
body:
# ssh-agent and ssh-add

`ssh-agent` keeps private keys available for repeated authentication without exposing them to every script as raw files and passphrases. `ssh-add` loads, lists, removes, locks, and constrains the identities stored in that agent.

## Start an Agent

```bash
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
```

This exports `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` and `SSH_AGENT_PID` into the current shell. Without `SSH_AUTH_SOCK`, `ssh-add` cannot talk to the agent.

## Add and Inspect Keys

```bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
ssh-add -t 1h -c ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
ssh-add -l
ssh-add -D
```

- `-t 1h` sets a lifetime.
- `-c` requires confirmation before each use.
- `-l` lists fingerprints of loaded identities.
- `-D` removes all loaded identities.

## Agent Forwarding Caution

```bash
ssh -A bastion.example.com
```

Agent forwarding is convenient but high-trust. A remote host that can reach your forwarded agent socket may request signatures with your loaded key. Use it only on trusted hops, consider destination constraints when supported, and prefer `ProxyJump` over `-A` when forwarding is unnecessary.

sections.title: Start ssh-agent and export environment
sections.body: Run `ssh-agent -s` and `eval` its output in the current shell so `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` and `SSH_AGENT_PID` are set. These variables tell OpenSSH and `ssh-add` where the agent socket lives. The wrapper script prints the agent startup output directly so callers can `eval` it.

sections.title: Load, inspect, and clear identities
sections.body: Use `ssh-add keyfile` to load a key, `ssh-add -l` to list fingerprints, `ssh-add -L` to list public keys, `ssh-add -d keyfile` to remove one key, and `ssh-add -D` to remove all keys. `ssh-add` ignores identity files that are accessible by others, so check file permissions if a key is unexpectedly skipped.

sections.title: Forwarding and constraints
sections.body: Forward agent access with `ssh -A` only when the remote hop is trusted. Newer OpenSSH releases support destination constraints with `ssh-add -h`, which limit where a forwarded key may be used, but forwarding still increases exposure compared with plain `ProxyJump`. Prefer no forwarding when the jump host only needs to pass traffic onward.

scripts.name: Manage ssh-agent
scripts.description: Start ssh-agent or manage loaded identities with ssh-add

SSH_AGENT_ACTION.description: Action: start, add, list, clear, remove, lock, or unlock

SSH_AGENT_LIFETIME.description: Default lifetime for ssh-agent -t when starting the agent

SSH_KEY_PATH.description: Key path for add or remove actions

SSH_ADD_LIFETIME.description: Lifetime for ssh-add -t when loading a key

SSH_ADD_CONFIRM.description: Set to true to require confirmation before key use

SSH_AUTH_SOCK.description: Agent socket used by non-start actions

keyPath.description: Private key path for add or remove

agentSocket.description: Custom socket path when starting the agent

addLifetime.description: Lifetime for a specific loaded key

confirm.description: Require confirmation before each key use

exitCode.description: Exit code from ssh-agent or ssh-add

depends.reason: ssh-agent manages existing keys; it does not create them
